Course: ML1, UChicago, Winter 2025

1. What We Built

I built this pipeline to answer a practical question: can we segment borrowers into cleaner, more risk-informative groups than a classical linear baseline can produce? I used 307,511 Home Credit applications, built a leakage-safe PCA baseline, then implemented SCARF from scratch to learn non-linear borrower embeddings, and finally compared clustering quality, risk separation, and stability on held-out test data.

Key result in one sentence: PCA won linear probe AUC (0.744918 vs 0.642574), but SCARF produced much tighter clusters and stronger risk stratification metrics (silhouette 0.385522 vs 0.179730, IV at chosen k 0.062542 vs 0.025499).

2. The Core Idea — SCARF

The SCARF idea is simple: I take one borrower row, corrupt part of it by swapping random features with values from other borrowers, and train a network so the original and corrupted versions land near each other while different borrowers stay far apart. This gives me a representation that captures stable identity-level structure rather than brittle raw-feature noise.

This is especially useful for tabular data because we do not have natural augmentations like flips or crops from computer vision. Random feature corruption is a tabular-native augmentation strategy, and if we respect categorical structure, it becomes a practical way to learn robust latent geometry.

Primary paper: SCARF (arXiv:2106.15147). Related directions I used for context: ReConTab (arXiv:2310.18516) and TCCL (2025 tabular contrastive work).

I chose SCARF over VAEs, deep clustering hybrids, and graph-heavy methods because I wanted a clean, controllable method that works directly on our mixed tabular features, scales to this dataset size, and keeps the story understandable for presentation and paper writing.

3. The Dataset

The data is Home Credit Default Risk from Kaggle: 307,511 applications and 122 raw columns (including target and id), with 120 raw predictive features before preprocessing. Default is only about 8.07%, so imbalance is strong from the start.

I’m working with mixed feature types: numeric financial signals like income, credit amount, annuity, and age proxies, plus categorical descriptors like education, occupation, and housing. Missingness is substantial; several columns exceed 70% missing, and missingness itself carries signal in credit data.

4. Notebook Map — Cell by Cell

This is the navigation map I want everyone to use when you pick up the notebook. If you need to debug or present one step, jump directly to that cell.

CellTypeWhat It Does
0MarkdownSets the project frame: borrower segmentation goal, motivation, related work, and the baseline-versus-SCARF roadmap.
1CodeImports all libraries, sets seeds/reproducibility flags, configures plotting defaults, and reports runtime device plus KneeLocator availability.
2MarkdownExplains the split-first, train-only preprocessing protocol and leakage controls before any modeling starts.
3CodeFinds and loads application_train.csv, separates target/id, and creates an 80/20 stratified train/test split.
4CodePrints dataset shape, dtype counts, sample head, and info/memory overview to validate raw data integrity.
5CodeRuns the full preprocessing pipeline: missingness indicators, DAYS_EMPLOYED anomaly fix, imputations, encoding, one-hot alignment, SCARF feature grouping, and scaling.
6CodePrints scaled feature summary and confirms class balance consistency in train and test splits.
7MarkdownIntroduces the EDA section and what questions the diagnostics are meant to answer.
8CodeBuilds the TARGET class-distribution bar chart and annotates percentage labels.
9CodePlots histograms for key numeric variables (income, credit, annuity, age proxy, employment days).
10CodeComputes TARGET correlations, draws the top-20 correlation heatmap, and prints ranked correlation values.
11CodeDraws class-conditional box plots for core financial/demographic variables on a sampled subset.
12CodeVisualizes missingness with a top-features bar chart and a sampled missingness heatmap.
13MarkdownIntroduces the PCA baseline and clarifies fair evaluation protocol for PCA, K-Means, hierarchical clustering, and DBSCAN.
14CodeDefines reusable helpers (sampled silhouette, bootstrap uncertainty, k-distance elbow) and fits PCA to 90% explained variance.
15CodeRuns K-Means model selection on PCA train features across k=2..10 using inertia and silhouette diagnostics.
16CodeFits final PCA K-Means on train and predicts test clusters; prints train/test cluster counts.
17CodeBuilds a Ward hierarchical dendrogram on a PCA test sample for structural comparison.
18CodeTunes PCA-space DBSCAN eps from k-distance elbow and reports cluster/noise statistics.
19MarkdownIntroduces manifold visualization goals for diagnosing overlap and separability in the baseline space.
20CodeCreates PCA-space UMAP views colored by TARGET, K-Means labels, and DBSCAN labels.
21CodeCreates PCA-space t-SNE views for additional non-linear geometry diagnostics.
22MarkdownAdds baseline interpretation commentary and motivation to move to a learned non-linear encoder.
23MarkdownIntroduces SCARF implementation choices and leakage-safe training protocol.
24CodeDefines SCARF dataset corruption logic, encoder/projection models, NT-Xent loss, optimizer schedule, and embedding extraction utilities.
25CodeTrains SCARF for 30 epochs on train data only and plots loss convergence over epochs.
26CodeExtracts 64-dimensional encoder embeddings for both train and test splits.
27MarkdownIntroduces apples-to-apples comparison protocol for SCARF clustering versus PCA baseline.
28CodeSelects SCARF K-Means k, evaluates test silhouette, and runs bootstrap silhouette comparison versus PCA.
29CodeBuilds SCARF-space UMAP visualizations with K-Means and TARGET overlays.
30CodeRuns DBSCAN in SCARF space on the same sampled rows as PCA and reports fair eps/noise comparison.
31CodeBuilds SCARF Ward dendrogram using the exact same sample indices used in PCA dendrogram.
32CodeComputes SCARF cluster profiles, produces profile/stability visualizations, and reports out-of-sample silhouette.
33MarkdownIntroduces the final interpretation section and metric reconciliation narrative.
34CodeComputes final comparison tables: AUC probes, silhouette metrics, IV at chosen/shared k, cluster size distributions, IV detail, and stability table.
35MarkdownSummarizes findings, business interpretation, limitations, future work, and references in prose.
36CodeExports deployment artifacts: encoder weights, preprocessing objects, feature schema, grouping metadata, and SCARF K-Means model.

5. Preprocessing — What I Did and Why

5.1 Split-First to Prevent Leakage

I split 80/20 with stratification before any imputing, encoding, scaling, or PCA fitting. That keeps all learned preprocessing statistics train-only and prevents the test set from leaking into feature engineering decisions.

5.2 DAYS_EMPLOYED Anomaly Fix

I explicitly handled the known placeholder value 365,243 in DAYS_EMPLOYED before median imputation. In this dataset it appears in 55,374 rows (18.01%), so leaving it untouched would distort medians and downstream scaling.

5.3 Missingness as Signal

For columns with more than 5% missingness in train, I added explicit missingness indicators. In credit data, missing can reflect process, customer behavior, or underwriting context, so this can be predictive and segment-informative.

5.4 Imputation and Encoding Choices

Numeric features are imputed with train medians because these distributions are skewed. Categorical features are imputed with train modes. I encoded NAME_EDUCATION_TYPE ordinally because it has meaningful order, while nominal categoricals are one-hot encoded.

5.5 Train/Test Column Alignment

For one-hot encoding, I force test columns to align exactly to the train schema. This avoids train/test mismatch and ensures stable inference behavior.

5.6 SCARF Group Semantics

I grouped all dummies from the same original categorical feature so SCARF corruption swaps whole categories, not single dummy bits. I also bundled each _MISSING indicator with its parent feature group so corruption preserves feature semantics instead of breaking them.

5.7 Scaling

I fit StandardScaler on train only and apply it to both splits. After transform, residual missing values are zero in both train and test. Final processed matrices are 246,008 by 282 (train) and 61,503 by 282 (test).

6. PCA Baseline

I fit PCA on train and selected the dimensionality that reaches about 90% explained variance. The selected dimension is 146 components.

When you run cell 15, it performs K-Means model selection on train PCA features using inertia and silhouette across k=2..10, then picks the best k by silhouette. The selected baseline k is 2.

I also ran hierarchical clustering and DBSCAN for structure checks beyond centroid-only assumptions.

Finally I used manifold visualization to inspect geometric separation.

7. SCARF — How It Works (No Code)

The encoder is a 3-layer neural network (256 to 128 to 64) that maps each borrower to a 64-number fingerprint. During training, a separate projection head (64 to 64 to 64) is used for contrastive loss, but at inference time I keep only the encoder output.

Corruption randomly selects 60% of logical features and swaps values with random other borrowers. For one-hot categoricals, I swap the whole group together so each corrupted sample stays semantically valid.

The NT-Xent objective pulls original and corrupted views together while pushing other borrowers apart. Training uses only the 246K-row train split for 30 epochs.

After training, I extract the 64-dimensional encoder embeddings and keep their magnitude. I do not unit-normalize at export because magnitude carries intensity information useful for clustering and profiling.

8. SCARF Results

I repeated the same clustering workflow in SCARF space. K-Means selection on train embeddings chooses k=9, and test silhouette jumps to 0.385522.

The UMAP projections are where the separation difference becomes visually obvious.

DBSCAN and hierarchical clustering were also rerun with fair sampling parity to PCA.

I profiled SCARF clusters using original-scale financial and external score features and checked train/test stability.

10. What This All Means — Honest Take

PCA wins on linear probe AUC (0.744918 vs 0.642574), which means its embedding is better aligned with a simple linear default boundary.

SCARF wins strongly on segmentation geometry (silhouette 0.385522 vs 0.179730, about 2.15x) and wins IV at richer granularity (shared k = 4, 6, 8 and chosen k).

My honest interpretation is that SCARF learned a better cluster-friendly geometry, but that geometry is not optimized for linear supervised probing. That tradeoff is exactly what contrastive representation learning can produce: better local structure, weaker linear separability.

For our project framing, this is acceptable and even useful, because our question is about borrower segmentation quality and risk stratification by clusters, not pure supervised leaderboard optimization.

11. Assumptions & Limitations

  1. I assume random train/test splitting is enough for validation, but temporal drift is not tested and could change cluster behavior in production.
  2. I assume TARGET labels are reliable, but label noise in repayment outcomes can blur cluster-risk interpretation.
  3. I assume one dataset (Home Credit) is representative; external validity to other lenders or geographies is unproven.
  4. I chose one SCARF architecture family (256 to 128 to 64) without broad architecture sweep, so better variants may exist.
  5. I set corruption rate to 0.6 by design choice, not exhaustive tuning; different rates could improve the AUC tradeoff.
  6. I use one random seed for the main reported run; multi-seed variance was not fully mapped.
  7. I use K-Means silhouette for k selection; alternative criteria or business constraints could select different k.
  8. I treat ordinal education encoding as valid ordering; any mismatch in true socio-economic ordering may inject bias.
  9. I rely on median/mode imputations; model-based imputations might preserve more structure.
  10. I use one-hot encoding for many categoricals, which can increase sparsity and may affect distance-based methods.
  11. I evaluate DBSCAN on sampled test subsets for feasibility; full-data DBSCAN could shift detailed cluster counts.
  12. I use UMAP/t-SNE for visualization diagnostics only; those projections can exaggerate or hide separations visually.
  13. I compare with a strong PCA baseline but not every modern tabular SSL method, so this is not a full benchmark study.
  14. I report linear probe AUC only; non-linear supervised probes were not added, so representation utility may be understated.
  15. I do not run nested cross-validation for all unsupervised steps; reported numbers should be treated as strong evidence, not final bounds.
  16. I assume missingness indicators are mostly helpful, but they can also encode process artifacts that vary by institution.
  17. I do not include fairness/disparate-impact analysis across protected groups, so ethical risk assessment is incomplete.

12. Deployment Artifacts

I exported 9 files in artifacts/ so the pipeline can be reproduced without retraining from scratch.

FileWhat It IsWhy It Matters
cat_group_indices.joblibFeature-group mapping for categorical corruption semanticsKeeps grouped categorical logic consistent with training-time assumptions.
categorical_impute_values.joblibMode values for categorical imputationsEnsures inference uses the same fill strategy as training.
feature_columns.joblibFinal processed column schemaGuarantees deterministic train/test/inference alignment.
label_encoders.joblibStored encoders for ordinal categorical fieldsPreserves category-to-integer mapping consistency.
numeric_impute_values.joblibMedian values for numeric imputationsPrevents recomputing statistics on future data.
pca_model.joblibFitted PCA objectRecreates baseline embeddings exactly.
scarf_encoder.ptTrained SCARF encoder weightsGenerates learned 64-dimensional borrower representations.
scarf_kmeans.joblibK-Means model fit in SCARF spaceAssigns borrowers to final SCARF clusters.
standard_scaler.joblibFitted StandardScalerKeeps feature scaling identical between training and inference.

12.1 Inference Pipeline (Practical Order)

  1. Load raw borrower row(s) and apply the same anomaly cleaning and imputation using saved train-fit statistics.
  2. Apply the same categorical encoding and strict feature-column alignment from feature_columns.joblib.
  3. Scale features with standard_scaler.joblib.
  4. Generate SCARF embedding with scarf_encoder.pt.
  5. Assign cluster with scarf_kmeans.joblib and read risk profile from cluster-level tables.

13. How to Run Everything

  1. Clone the project: git clone https://github.com/manuelarceaguirre/home-credit-scarf.git and move into the folder.
  2. Create and activate a virtual environment: python3 -m venv .venv then source .venv/bin/activate (macOS/Linux) or .venv\Scripts\activate (Windows).
  3. Install notebook dependencies used in the project (pandas, numpy, scikit-learn, scipy, umap-learn, matplotlib, seaborn, torch, tqdm, kneed, jupyter).
  4. Place application_train.csv in the project root (or update the data path candidates in the notebook).
  5. Run the notebook top to bottom: jupyter notebook home_credit_scarf.ipynb, then execute all cells in order 0 through 36.
  6. If you only need final figures and tables quickly, open home_credit_scarf_executed.ipynb and reuse the stored outputs.
  7. To modify experiments, start with cell 5 (preprocessing knobs), cell 25 (SCARF hyperparameters), and cell 28/cell 34 (evaluation settings and reporting tables).

14. What Needs to Happen Next

14.1 Presentation Plan

I recommend we structure slides around one clear narrative: problem framing, leakage-safe setup, baseline geometry, SCARF geometry, and honest metric tradeoff.

  1. Start with the imbalance and missingness figures (Figures 1, 5, 6) to justify preprocessing rigor.
  2. Show baseline geometry with PCA UMAP/t-SNE (Figures 11, 12) and explain overlap.
  3. Show SCARF geometry and silhouette bootstrap (Figures 15 to 18) to make the gain visually undeniable.
  4. Close with the summary table and shared-k IV line (Section 9 + Figure 22) to answer fairness questions proactively.

14.2 Paper Draft Mapping

  1. Introduction and motivation: cells 0, 7, 13, 23.
  2. Data and preprocessing methodology: cells 2 through 6 plus EDA cells 8 through 12.
  3. Baseline methodology/results: cells 14 through 21.
  4. SCARF methodology/results: cells 24 through 32.
  5. Comparative evaluation and discussion: cells 34 and 35.
  6. Reproducibility/deployment appendix: cell 36 and artifacts section.

14.3 Suggested Teammate Focus

  • Wade Chen: baseline pipeline explanation (PCA, K-Means, DBSCAN tuning logic, and why baseline matters).
  • Rita Luo: SCARF core intuition and architecture/training story for non-specialist clarity.
  • Qiqi Huang: quantitative evaluation sections (silhouette, IV tables, shared-k fairness argument).
  • Nick Dhaliwal: EDA and preprocessing decisions (imbalance, missingness, leakage prevention choices).
  • Yingqi Chen: business interpretation and limitations/future-work framing.
  • Manuel (me): integrate final narrative flow, answer methods questions, and align paper text with slide story.

14.4 Talking Points I’d Use Verbatim

  • "Our objective was segmentation quality, not only supervised default prediction, so geometry metrics are first-class outcomes."
  • "PCA is better for a linear probe in this run, but SCARF builds a much more clusterable latent space."
  • "Shared-k IV checks show the gain is not just from selecting more clusters."
  • "The pipeline is leakage-safe and artifact-exported, so this is reproducible and deployment-ready for cluster assignment."
